topic: should we implement streaming speech bubbles for the congress page, or ship it as-is and iterate?

rounds.response: Ship it as-is. Streaming speech bubbles are a UI flourish — they add complexity to both the rendering layer and the data pipeline, and you don't yet have real usage data to tell you whether users even *want* that experience. The failure modes (partial renders, out-of-order updates, broken state on reconnect) are non-trivial. Get the baseline in front of people first, then let actual behavior tell you if the wait time is painful enough to warrant the investment.

Ship it. Streaming speech bubbles are a nice-to-have that will balloon into a week of WebSocket plumbing, state management edge cases, and cross-browser audio sync nightmares. You don't even know yet if users *want* that — you're solving a problem you haven't confirmed exists. Get real users on it first, then add streaming if the data says it matters.

Ship it. Streaming speech bubbles are a *nice-to-have* that adds real complexity — and right now you don't even know if users will engage with the page enough to care. Get it in front of real people first; if they're reading long enough to feel the wait, *then* you've earned the right to build streaming. Shipping fast gives you signal. Polishing in the dark gives you assumptions.

Ship it as-is. Streaming speech bubbles are a UI polish concern, and polish before validation is how teams waste sprints — you don't know yet if the congress page *works* for users, let alone whether they'll sit there long enough to appreciate animated text. The iteration argument isn't laziness here, it's correct sequencing: get real feedback on the interaction model first, then spend engineering time on the thing people actually complained was slow or confusing. Any persona arguing for streaming now needs to show me a user pain point, not an aesthetic preference.

verdict: Ship it as-is. Streaming speech bubbles are a UI polish concern, and polish before validation is how teams waste sprints — you don't know yet if the congress page *works* for users, let alone whether they'll sit there long enough to appreciate animated text. The iteration argument isn't laziness her
